origin: A passage from the New Testament—specifically the farewell/discourse section of the Gospel of John—where Jesus reassures his disciples before his departure and frames faith in the face of persecution (“In the world you have tribulation, but take courage…”).
author: Most likely the evangelist/author traditionally associated with the Gospel of John (often attributed to John the Apostle, though modern scholarship typically treats the Gospel as written by an unknown Johannine author/redactor). The immediate speaker within the text is Jesus.
thoughts: The author’s (or speaker’s) perspective is pastoral and exhortative: acknowledging that believers will experience hardship in the “world,” but motivating them with confidence—because victory over the world is already achieved. The motivation appears to be strengthening morale and preserving faith under pressure by grounding hope in a promised/assured divine triumph.
audience: Disciples/early believers who are facing or anticipating distress and opposition; broader Christian readers looking for encouragement to remain steadfast.
written_when: The original sayings are situated in the historical period of Jesus (1st century CE), while the Gospel of John is generally dated to the late 1st century, around ~90–100 CE.
